narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 49.7% chance that you would fail it. This threshold is a key part of our admission process, as we aim to select students with the strongest projected outcomes based on patterns from past bar takers. The model's decision hinged primarily on three factors from your profile: your Year 3 grade decile, your undergraduate GPA, and your LSAT score. Most influential was your Year 3 decile of 2, which ranks you relatively low in your school's performance distribution—far below the average of about 6 for those who passed the bar—and this strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting failure. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 worked in your favor, as it was close to the 3.24 average among bar passers, helping to pull the odds slightly toward success. Similarly, your LSAT score of 37 offered a modest boost, nearly matching the 37.27 average for successful students and countering some of the risk. While your high family income and full-time enrollment status align well with typical admitted students—where high income is uncommon but full-time is the norm at 93%—they played smaller roles here compared to your academic deciles and scores. Overall, your Year 3 performance stood out as the biggest concern, placing you below the profiles of most accepted applicants who typically rank much higher in later-year deciles. This doesn't reflect your potential or effort, but rather how these metrics correlated with bar success in our data. To strengthen a future application, focus on boosting your later-year grades to climb those deciles, perhaps through targeted study habits or academic support. Retaking the LSAT for a higher score could also tip the scales, given its positive but limited influence. We encourage you to reapply after addressing these areas, as many students improve dramatically with focused effort. Your path to law school is still very much open.
